access.entityLabel: British Library, Harley MS 2324

access.accessMethod: Request the shelfmark through the British Library Reading Rooms item-request form; registration and eligibility rules apply.
access.entityLabel: British Library, Harley MS 2324

access.accessMethod: First ask whether an existing complete digital surrogate of Harley MS 2324 is available. If none exists, request a quote for expert full-color photography of the entire physical codex: covers and boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves and endleaves, blanks, stubs, and every recto and verso folio or other surface. Separately identify ff. 1r-128v as the target-work portion. Only if the complete codex is infeasible should an exact-folio or partial order be considered.
access.contact: British Library digitisation order form; licensing@bl.uk for intended publication or reuse questions
access.entityLabel: British Library, Harley MS 2324
access.format: Expert photography; exact deliverables and quote subject to provider assessment
notes: The service says precious or rare manuscripts require expert photography rather than ordinary scanning.
The price list supplies per-image rates but does not establish the final number of photographs, handling category, image format, delivery terms, or total quote for this manuscript.
This request strategy does not imply service approval, feasibility, a total price, or permission to reuse or publish delivered images; obtain those terms in writing separately.

dateStatements.label: Production
dateStatements.value: First quarter of the fifteenth century

entities.description: The complete first-quarter-fifteenth-century parchment witness used throughout Swinburn's edition.
entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 2324
notes: Swinburn milestones use recto/verso as a/b, from 1a through 128b.
No current public whole-codex image service was found.
The work occupies ff. 1r-128v.
Small red initials; Latin quotations underlined in red according to Swinburn; post-1600 binding.

access.accessMethod: No surviving object is known; consult the trial evidence and later scholarly discussion.
access.entityLabel: Reported copy of The Lanterne of Light owned by John Claydon

entities.description: A red-leather parchment book in a good English hand, copied for John Claydon by John Grime and used as evidence at Claydon's 1415 heresy trial.

holding.notes: Historical ownership only; no present holding exists.

entities.label: Reported copy of The Lanterne of Light owned by John Claydon
notes: Claydon was burned in September 1415, and the condemned book is not among the two manuscript copies identified as surviving by the British Library; its precise loss history is not established here.
No descent relation to Harley 2324, Harley 6613, or the Redman print is asserted.
Trial evidence described a good English hand.
Its exact contents and readings cannot be recovered from the surviving reports.

rights.basis: This record addresses the reported physical manuscript's historical custody and loss only; no modern repository or surviving object is known. Rights in the underlying medieval text are a distinct layer. This physical-object record grants no license for any image, facsimile, or reproduction that may later be identified.
rights.component: Historical physical-material ownership, custody, survival, and access record for John Claydon's reported manuscript

next_steps: First ask the British Library whether an existing complete digital surrogate, including any legacy or unpublished surrogate, exists for either shelfmark.

If no complete surrogate exists, request the whole physical codex in color, including covers and boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves and endleaves, blanks, stubs, and every recto and verso folio or other surface, rather than only the work portion.
Associate the target work with Harley 2324 ff. 1r-128v and Harley 6613 ff. 1r-57v inside the complete requested objects.
Use an exact-folio or partial order only as a fallback if a complete-codex surrogate is infeasible.
Ask for written reuse and publication terms before placing any delivered photographs in a public repository.
notes: Legacy FullDisplay and Viewer URLs now redirect to a generic British Library digitised-collections page rather than exposing item images.
openQuestions.question: Can complete full-color digital facsimiles of Harley MSS 2324 and 6613 be obtained or made publicly accessible?

next_steps: Collate both surviving manuscripts and the complete Redman state.
Consult recent stemmatic and Lollard transmission scholarship.
Do not add direct descent edges without explicit evidence.
openQuestions.question: What are the reading-level relationships among Harley MSS 2324 and 6613, the Redman print, and the reported Claydon copy?

next_steps: Inspect the EEBO record through a subscribing institution for source-library and shelfmark metadata.
Compare image imperfections against the Folger copy before linking the image set to that physical copy.
openQuestions.question: Which physical copy was filmed for EEBO microfilm 103:06 and image set 8704?

next_steps: Ask James M. Dean or Medieval Institute Publications whether the editorial files survive.
Reconcile the selection locator ff. 1v-4r, 5r-20v with the acknowledgments' ff. 1r-4r, 5v-13r before asserting exact reproduction coverage.
openQuestions.question: What folios were actually present in the British Library electrostatic print used by Dean?

next_steps: Monitor the official dean-political repository for an XML or TEI addition.
Ask METS Editions whether an unpublished XML conversion exists or is planned.
If an exact XML file appears, cache the pinned provider bytes and apply the provider's XML license only to that identified deliverable.
openQuestions.question: Will METS Editions publish an exact XML or TEI file for Dean's currently legacy-HTML edition?

next_steps: Ask Kyvero Press and U-M whether the data file is intended to fall under the CME software license or U-M's No Copyright-United States assertion.
Document any response separately from rights in the medieval text and the 1917 edition.
Do not infer that the root repository's output license automatically licenses inherited source data.
openQuestions.question: What license governs redistribution and commercial reuse of the inherited AHA2749 XML encoding and its editorial markup?

next_steps: Determine Swinburn's death date and apply the law of each intended jurisdiction.
Obtain item-specific permission or terms for modern manuscript photographs, Internet Archive derivatives, and METS files before redistribution.
openQuestions.question: What is the current non-U.S. copyright status of Swinburn's 1917 editorial contribution and what provider terms would govern redistribution of each scan or reproduction?

reviewNotes: The anonymous medieval work is distinct from every manuscript, print, edition, scan, encoding, and repository artifact represented here.
Harley MS 2324 is Swinburn's complete base witness. Harley MS 6613 is collateral and is not represented as a source of Swinburn's text.
The exact textual relationships among the two surviving manuscripts, the Redman print, and Claydon's reported copy require collation and are not inferred from shared work identity.
John Wycliffe is an excluded historical attribution: the work's dependence on the 1409 Constitutions postdates his death in 1384.
John Grime copied Claydon's lost book; Robert Redman printed the later edition. Neither activity establishes medieval authorship.
The Folger record describes one imperfect physical copy of STC 15225, but the exact physical exemplar behind EEBO image set 8704 remains unresolved.
Dean's published selection locator and his acknowledgments' electrostatic-print folio ranges conflict; the intermediary's exact coverage is therefore left unresolved.
The Dean edition is currently labeled Legacy HTML. Its pinned official repository contains only a reduced PDF, README, and LICENSE, so no exact public Dean XML deliverable is asserted.
Public-domain determinations are jurisdiction-specific. Modern photography, editorial contributions, provider interfaces, and contractual terms remain separate.
British Library catalog text is CC BY 4.0; that license does not cover the manuscripts or future reproduction photographs.
The CME submodule's LICENSE expressly speaks of software, while the root README discusses code and output; neither clearly resolves reuse of this inherited XML data artifact.

sourceLinks.accessMethod: First ask whether an existing complete digital surrogate of Harley MS 2324 is available. If none exists, request a quote for expert full-color photography of the entire physical codex: covers and boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves and endleaves, blanks, stubs, and every recto and verso folio or other surface. Separately identify ff. 1r-128v as the target-work portion. Only if the complete codex is infeasible should an exact-folio or partial order be considered.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a pinned repository copy of University of Michigan CME item AHA2749. U-M encoded selected runs of Lilian M. Swinburn's 1917 EETS edition: title page, contents, introduction, the complete edited text on pp. 1-137, appendix, and notes, but not the glossary on pp. 153-192. Swinburn transcribed the complete treatise from British Library Harley MS 2324, ff. 1r-128v; Harley MS 6613 is a distinct, imperfect collateral witness. A reported parchment copy made for John Claydon was condemned in 1415 and does not survive. A circa-1535 Robert Redman print survives as a later transmission state and has a separate EEBO-TCP encoding. James M. Dean's 1996 edition supplies a later partial scholarly path based on an electrostatic print of Harley 2324 and checked against Swinburn. Cached deliverables preserve the pinned CME XML and license, the complete public Swinburn scan, official catalog metadata, the complete TCP text, the complete Dean anthology carrier, pinned METS repository evidence, and rights and reproduction-service evidence; no current public manuscript facsimile was found for either surviving Harley witness.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous; historically attributed to John Wycliffe, but that attribution is rejected by the work's post-1409 date
catalogSummary.date: Probably 1409-1415; first securely attested 17 August 1415; first known print circa 1535; source edition 1917
catalogSummary.region: England; precise place of composition unknown; Harley MS 2324 has East Midland language with a few Northern features
catalogSummary.title: The Lanterne of Light

catalogingSubject.label: The Lanterne of Light
catalogingSubject.scope_note: One anonymous Middle English Lollard prose treatise in thirteen numbered chapters, composed after Archbishop Arundel's 1409 Constitutions and attested at John Claydon's 1415 heresy trial. The work is distinct from Harley MSS 2324 and 6613, the reported-lost Claydon copy, the circa-1535 Redman print, Swinburn's 1917 edition, Dean's 1996 partial edition, the U-M encoding, and the repository XML. AHA2749 encodes Swinburn's title page, contents, introduction, complete edited text, appendix, and notes, but omits the printed glossary.

contentParts.label: Swinburn title page and imprint
contentParts.summary: Editorial title page for the 1917 EETS edition.

contentParts.label: Contents
contentParts.summary: Swinburn's contents list, including the glossary that AHA2749 does not otherwise encode.

contentParts.label: Introduction
contentParts.summary: Swinburn's discussion of authorship and date, manuscript description, and grammar.

contentParts.label: The Lanterne of Light
contentParts.summary: The complete anonymous prose treatise in thirteen numbered chapters.

contentParts.label: Chapter I: Prologue
contentParts.summary: Prologue on steadfastness in truth during tribulation and on spiritual understanding of Scripture.

contentParts.label: Chapter II: Of a peticioun
contentParts.summary: A petition for readers' prayers that the work may be completed to God's honor and servants' profit.

contentParts.label: Chapter III: What is anticrist in general wiþ VI condiciouns
contentParts.summary: Defines Antichrist generally through six moral conditions.

contentParts.label: Chapter IV: What is anticrist in special wiþ hise þre parties
contentParts.summary: Treats Antichrist specifically through three institutional parts.

contentParts.label: Chapter V: What is anticrist in special wiþ fyue condiciouns
contentParts.summary: Further identifies Antichrist through five conditions and prophetic signs.

contentParts.label: Chapter VI: What is þe chirche oonli proprid to God wiþ hir names
contentParts.summary: Defines the true church belonging properly to God and explains its names and qualities.

contentParts.label: Chapter VII: What is þe material chirche wiþ hir honourmentis
contentParts.summary: Describes the material church and critiques its buildings, ornaments, and religious institutions.

contentParts.label: Chapter VIII: Of good and yuel comyng to þis material chirche
contentParts.summary: Contrasts the good and evil people gathered in the material church.

contentParts.label: Chapter IX: Of discrecioun to knowe þe good from þe yuel
contentParts.summary: Teaches spiritual discernment between good and evil members of the mixed church.

contentParts.label: Chapter X: How þe good of þe secounde chirche acorden wiþ þe firste chirche
contentParts.summary: Explains how faithful members of the material church agree with the true church through faith, hope, and charity.

contentParts.label: Chapter XI: Of Ioye in tribulacioun
contentParts.summary: Exhorts persecuted believers to patience and joy in tribulation.

contentParts.label: Chapter XII: Of þe fendis cautels and þe kepers of Goddis heestis
contentParts.summary: The longest chapter applies the Ten Commandments against contemporary religious, legal, economic, and social abuses.

contentParts.label: Chapter XIII: What is þe fendis chirche wiþ hir propurtees
contentParts.summary: Defines the devil's church, catalogs its properties, and closes with an appeal to turn from sin and receive everlasting life.

contentParts.label: Appendix: Sources of the Quotations from the Bible
contentParts.summary: Swinburn's analysis of Latin biblical sources, English translation, and its relation to Wycliffite Bible versions.

contentParts.label: Notes
contentParts.summary: Swinburn's textual, lexical, historical, and source notes.

assertion.notes: The treatise responds to Arundel's 1409 Constitutions and was identified at Claydon's trial in August 1415. Swinburn favored 1409-1410; Dean gives the broader 1409-1415 window used here.

dateStatements.display: 17 August 1415, at John Claydon's heresy trial

assertion.notes: The cataloged date is conjectural, conventionally represented as 1535?.

dateStatements.display: Circa 1535, printed in London by Robert Redman

extent.description: One prose work in thirteen numbered chapters. AHA2749 contains three DIV1, eighteen DIV2, nineteen DIV3, four DIV4, 330 P, 164 PB, and 256 MILESTONE elements. Its four LG and 23 L elements are confined to Swinburn's editorial appendix and notes and are excluded from the work-form counts.

assertion.notes: The XML's 23 L elements and four LG elements occur only in Swinburn's appendix and notes; they do not make the medieval work mixed prose and verse.

formStatements.description: Continuous prose treatise in a prologue, petition, and eleven further numbered chapters.

scope.description: Biblical, patristic, canon-law, and scholastic quotations embedded throughout the Middle English prose

notes: Harley MS 2324 is Swinburn's complete base witness; Harley MS 6613 is a distinct imperfect collateral witness and is not represented as a source of the 1917 edition.
The reported Claydon copy was a red-leather parchment book copied by John Grime and condemned in 1415; its readings and relation to surviving witnesses are unknown.
The Folger catalog's historical associations with John Grime and William Hardy belong to the circa-1535 print record and are not adopted as confirmed medieval authorship.
The glossary on printed pp. 153-192 is present in the cached full Swinburn scan but absent from AHA2749.

next_steps: Consult recent authorship and Lollard textual-network scholarship.
Keep John Wycliffe, John Grime, and William Hardy as attribution-history or transmission-layer names unless new evidence supports authorship.
openQuestions.question: Can the anonymous author be identified more closely than a participant in the early-fifteenth-century Lollard milieu?

next_steps: Consult dialect studies across both surviving witnesses and the early print.
Do not promote Swinburn's East Midland witness-language finding to a composition-place claim.
openQuestions.question: Where was the work composed, as distinct from the dialect and production place of Harley MS 2324?

next_steps: Collate the complete surviving witnesses and early print.
Do not infer direct descent among these states from shared work identity alone.
openQuestions.question: What are the exact textual relationships among Harley MSS 2324 and 6613, the circa-1535 Redman print, and the reported Claydon copy?

assertion.notes: This broad English context is not a claim for a precise composition site.
Claydon's London ownership and trial establish attestation, not composition in London.
Swinburn describes an East Midland dialect with a few Northern features. A witness's copied language does not prove the author's place of composition.

scope.description: Language of Harley MS 2324 as edited by Swinburn

assertion.notes: The author is anonymous; neither a surviving copyist nor a later printer is treated as the work's creator.
This records attribution history, not support for Wycliffe's authorship. The work cites the 1409 Constitutions and therefore postdates Wycliffe's death in 1384.

notes: Swinburn transcribed Harley MS 2324, expanded contractions in italics, modernized capitalization, generally retained punctuation, and supplied introduction, appendix, notes, and glossary.

scope.description: Immediate 1917 source edition and its editorial frame

summaries.value: The treatise identifies Antichrist first in general moral terms and then in the pope, prelates, and religious orders; distinguishes the true church, the material mixed church, and the devil's church; teaches discernment, faith, hope, charity, and joy under persecution; and applies the Ten Commandments to contemporary religious and social abuses. Middle English exposition is repeatedly grounded in Latin Scripture and patristic, scholastic, and canon-law authorities.

The work is prose-only. Verse-line markup in AHA2749 belongs to quotations in Swinburn's appendix and notes, not to the medieval treatise. The XML reproduces Swinburn's complete edited text but not his glossary, and its legacy Wycliffe author field records superseded attribution history.

assertion.notes: Modern display form of the established title; it is not claimed as an authorial manuscript rubric.

scope.description: Title of the circa-1535 Redman print state

titles.value: Here begynnethe the lanterne of lyght
God þat is good in him silf, faire in hise aungelis, merveilouse in hise seintis, and merciful vpon synners
God wole not þat ȝe be deed, but þat ȝe haue euerelastyng lijf

work.author: Anonymous; historically attributed to John Wycliffe, but that attribution is rejected by the work's post-1409 date
work.dateDisplay: Probably 1409-1415; first securely attested 17 August 1415; first known print circa 1535; source edition 1917
work.regionDisplay: England; precise place of composition unknown; Harley MS 2324 has East Midland language with a few Northern features
work.summary: The treatise identifies Antichrist first in general moral terms and then in the pope, prelates, and religious orders; distinguishes the true church, the material mixed church, and the devil's church; teaches discernment, faith, hope, charity, and joy under persecution; and applies the Ten Commandments to contemporary religious and social abuses. Middle English exposition is repeatedly grounded in Latin Scripture and patristic, scholastic, and canon-law authorities.
